ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed fish food product class; no specific entry for steamed whole seabass with garlic, lime and chili exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sea bass (Lates calcarifer / Dicentrarchus labrax) — steamed, as closest FoodEx2 fish preparation category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for steamed whole seabass with garlic, lime and chili. A0CKP (sea bass) used as closest parent; condiment components not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 covers steamed fish generically; no specific code for seabass with garlic, lime and chili exists.

serving.description: 1 whole fish portion (approximately 400–600 g whole fish, yielding ~250 g edible flesh with sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one whole steamed seabass (approximately 400–600 g whole fish) shared among 2–3 diners at a Zi Char restaurant, yielding roughly 250 g of edible flesh and sauce per diner.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed fish (sea bass, Lates calcarifer) with condiments — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (sea bass, raw, cooked by moist heat) adapted with garlic, lime, chili sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient base derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for steamed sea bass (ikan siakap kukus) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15060 (sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) for macro validation. Garlic, lime juice, and chili contribute minor macro adjustments estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data. Values represent the edible portion per 100 g including sauce.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant fish dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed fish and high-protein low-carbohydrate seafood dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed whole seabass with garlic, lime and chili. GI is estimated at 5 based on the University of Sydney GI Database convention for high-protein, very low-carbohydrate fish dishes (carbohydrate_g ~2.2 per 100 g). Pure protein and fat foods have negligible glycemic response. GI of 5 is consistent with published analogous steamed fish entries. GL per 250 g serving = (5 × 5.5 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~310 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the sodium contributed by light soy sauce in the sauce; requesting reduced-soy or sauce-on-the-side preparation can lower sodium intake to a more acceptable level for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.2 g per 100 g) and high lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed seabass with garlic, lime and chili is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index (~5), minimal carbohydrate load, and high lean protein content that supports satiety without causing postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein, low saturated fat, omega-3 fatty acids from seabass
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed seabass is a heart-healthy option providing lean protein and beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids with low saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g); the steaming method avoids added cooking fats, though sodium from soy sauce should be moderated in individuals with concurrent hypertension and CVD risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Whole Seabass with Garlic, Lime and Chili is a classic Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char (煮炒) seafood dish in which a whole Asian seabass (Lates calcarifer, locally known as ikan siakap) is steamed over high heat until the flesh is tender and flaky, then dressed with a fragrant sauce of minced garlic, fresh lime juice, sliced red chili, light soy sauce, sesame oil, and garnished with spring onion and ginger. The dish is celebrated for its clean, bright flavour profile — the natural sweetness of the seabass is complemented by the sharp acidity of lime, the pungency of garlic, and the mild heat of chili. The flesh is moist, delicate, and easily separable from the bone. Nutritionally, this dish is a high-protein, low-fat, low-carbohydrate option providing approximately 105 kcal per 100 g, 18.5 g protein, and only 2.2 g carbohydrate, making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or those following low-glycemic dietary patterns. The glycemic index is negligible (~5) due to the near-absence of digestible carbohydrates. Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) is moderate and primarily derived from soy sauce; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation. The dish is naturally halal, dairy-free, and rich in vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. It is commonly served as a shared main course at Zi Char restaurants and seafood houses across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Calories in steamed whole seabass with garlic lime chili
Is steamed fish good for diabetics?
Best fish for weight loss Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (steamed sea bass / ikan siakap kukus) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for sea bass cooked by moist heat. No single approved source provides an exact match for the full dish including garlic, lime and chili sauce components. Minor macro adjustments for sauce ingredients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database conventions for high-protein, very low-carbohydrate fish dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the edible portion per 100 g including sauce dressing. Whole fish yield (edible flesh as percentage of whole fish weight) is approximately 45–55% for seabass. 2. Sodium content (310 mg per 100 g) reflects typical Zi Char preparation with light soy sauce; home or reduced-sodium preparations may have significantly lower sodium. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as steaming with minimal soy-based sauce contains no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy and MyFCD source data. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy sea bass cooked moist heat data. 5. GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 250 g serving = 0.3. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 is used as closest parent (steamed fish food product); no exact FoodOn entry for steamed whole seabass with garlic, lime and chili exists. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest parent for sea bass; the full condiment preparation is not separately coded in FoodEx2. 8. Vitamin C (4.5 mg) reflects contribution from lime juice and chili in the sauce. 9. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated or sourced numeric values where defensible data exists; no -1 values remain in this record.
